elements.sent-2411-001: Recent experiments with quantum simulators using ultracold atoms and superconducting qubits have demonstrated the potential of controlled dissipation as a versatile tool for realizing correlated many-body states.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, determining the dynamics of dissipative quantum many-body systems remains a significant analytical and numerical challenge.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we focus on a dissipative impurity problem as a testbed for new methodological developments.
elements.sent-2411-004: We introduce an efficient non-perturbative framework that combines the superposition of Gaussian states (SGS) variational ansatz with the quantum trajectory approach to simulate open systems featuring a dissipative impurity.
elements.sent-2411-005: Applying this method to a spinful impurity subject to two-body losses and embedded in a bath of noninteracting fermions, we explore the full crossover from weak to strong dissipation regimes.
elements.sent-2411-006: The non-perturbative nature of the SGS ansatz allows us to thoroughly examine this crossover, providing comprehensive insights into the system's behavior.
elements.sent-2411-007: In the strong dissipation regime, our approach reproduces the finding that localized two-body losses can induce the Kondo effect [arXiv:2406.03527], characterized by a slowdown of spin relaxation and an enhancement of charge conductance.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, we reveal an exotic ``reverse conductance" phenomenon at zero potential bias -- a counter-intuitive single-body effect resulting from intermediate dissipation and finite bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-009: Finally, we investigate the formation of ferromagnetic domains and propose an extension to realize a higher-spin Kondo model using localized dissipation.
elements.sent-2411-010: Dissipative quantum physics has been an active area of research for several decades.
elements.sent-2411-011: Coupling a quantum system to an environment not only acts detrimentally by inducing decoherence and dephasing, but can also result in the onset of phase transitions, as in the celebrated case of the spin-boson model \cite{Leggett1987 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: With the advent of ultracold quantum simulators, it has been realized that dissipation can be used as a tool for preparing correlated states of matter \cite{kraus2008,diehl2008quantum,verstraete2009,lin2013,bardyn2013,shankar2013,Nakagawa2020,raghunandan2020,sieberer2023universality (see also recent work on quantum simulators using superconducting qubits \cite{ma2019,Mi2024 ).}}
elements.sent-2411-013: These developments underscore that dissipation, while typically suppressing quantum coherence, can also give rise to novel phenomena without counterparts in unitary systems, such as non-equilibrium critical steady states \cite{dalla2010,dallatorre2012 and instances of quantum many-body Zeno physics \cite{misra1977,fisher2001,facchi2002,syassen2008,facchi2008,Sun2023 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: Understanding dissipative systems poses significant analytical and numerical challenges.
elements.sent-2411-015: Various simulation methods have been employed, including perturbative techniques like the Keldysh formalism \cite{Sieberer2013,torre2013,Sieberer2016,Maghrebi2016,gievers2023 and non-perturbative approaches such as exact diagonalization (ED) \cite{Cai2013,PhysRevE.92.042143,Nakagawa2020 , density matrix renormalization group (DMRG) \cite{Daley2009,bonnes2014,yamamoto2022 , matrix product operators (MPOs) \cite{Mascarenhas2015,Cui2015 , Bethe ansatz \cite{buca2020,nakagawa2021,alba2023,ekman2024 , and variational approaches \cite{hartmann2019,bond2024 .}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: However, non-perturbative methods face significant challenges when applied to large non-integrable dissipative systems, since the dimension of the space of density matrices grows with the size of the system even faster than for pure states.
elements.sent-2411-017: Thus, an important goal of theoretical quantum physics has been the development of approximations that are still non-perturbative but are less computationally intensive.
elements.sent-2411-018: A class of dissipative systems garnering increasing attention is that of dissipative impurities \cite{tonielli2019orthogonality,Ott_dissipative_imp,Dolgirev,tonielli2020ramsey,chaudhari2022zeno,gievers2023 , where dissipation is confined to a small region within a larger quantum system.}
elements.sent-2411-019: As their unitary counterparts (such as the Kondo \cite{Kondo and Anderson impurity models \cite{anderson1961 and polarons \cite{Landau1933,pekar1946 , to mention only a few), they represent an ideal playground to understand the effect of dissipation in a simplified setting.}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In this article, we present a non-perturbative framework that combines the superposition of Gaussian states (SGS) variational ansatz---which is efficient for impurity problems \cite{BravyiGosset_complexity,boutin2021quantum,nondissipativeSGS ---with the quantum trajectory approach \cite{Dalibard1992,Molmer1993,Dum1992,Dum1992_2,carmichael1993open .}}
elements.sent-2411-021: This framework significantly accelerates numerical simulations of the dynamics in dissipative impurity systems and holds potential for application to other platforms featuring local dissipation acting on an extended quantum system.
elements.sent-2411-022: We apply our method to a dissipative impurity system where a spinful impurity, immersed in a bath of noninteracting fermions, undergoes two-body losses.
elements.sent-2411-023: Our approach effectively demonstrates that strong localized two-body losses give rise to the Kondo effect \cite{Hewson , in agreement with the analysis presented in the companion paper \cite{short_paper .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: We characterize the emergence of Kondo physics by identifying two distinctive signatures: the slowdown of spin relaxation and the enhancement of charge conductance.
elements.sent-2411-025: Owing to the non-perturbative nature of the SGS ansatz, our method allows for a thorough examination of the full crossover from the weakly dissipative, mean-field regime to the strongly correlated Kondo regime, providing a comprehensive understanding of the system's behavior across different dissipation regimes.
elements.sent-2411-026: Notably, in the mean-field regime, we reveal the emergence of exotic ``reverse conductance" at zero potential bias, arising from intermediate dissipation and the constraints of a finite reservoir bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-027: Furthermore, we explore a straightforward extension of the dissipative setup by introducing dissipation to multiple sites, enabling the realization of a spin-$1$ Kondo model.
elements.sent-2411-028: This development opens up new avenues for exploring higher-spin Kondo physics utilizing dissipation.
elements.sent-2411-029: The paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-030: In Sec.~\ref{sec: model , we present the dissipative impurity model with two-body losses.}
elements.sent-2411-031: Sec.~\ref{sec: method introduces the SGS framework for simulating open systems, which is combined with the quantum trajectory approach.}
elements.sent-2411-032: In Sec.~\ref{sec: results , we first benchmark our approach using ED.}
elements.sent-2411-033: We then investigate the crossover to Kondo regimes through spin relaxation and conductance.
elements.sent-2411-034: In Sec.~\ref{sec: negative conductance , we demonstrate the origin of the ``reverse conductance" phenomenon at zero potential bias.}
elements.sent-2411-035: In Sec.~\ref{sec: high spin we discuss the formation of ferromagnetic domains and the realization of high-spin Kondo models.}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=0.9\linewidth]{Figures/model.pdf} \caption{Schematic of the setup with two-body dissipation at an impurity site, which is immersed in a spinful fermionic bath consisting of multiple channels. } \label{fig: setup}
elements.sent-2411-036: We consider an impurity site immersed in a bath of noninteracting spinful fermions.
elements.sent-2411-037: As illustrated in Fig.~\ref{fig: setup , fermions on the central impurity site, can undergo a two-body loss: when two fermions with opposite spins occupy this site, they can be lost from the system at a rate $\gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-038: Meanwhile, they can also tunnel into the bath, which consists of multiple channels (depicted by the cyan balls in Fig.~\ref{fig: setup ).}
elements.sent-2411-039: Assuming the losses are Markovian \cite{DipolarMolecules2BLoss, PRL_2BLoss_Zeno, DissipativeFermiHubbard,Esslinger-PhysRevA.100.053605 , the dynamics of the density matrix $\rho(t)$ of the system is described by a Lindblad master equation \begin{equation}\label{eq: master equation} \dot{\rho}(t)=-\ii\comm{H}{\rho(t)}+\gamma\Big(L\rho(t)L^\dag-\frac{1}{2}\{L^\dag L,\rho(t)\}\Big),\end{equation} where the unitary part of the dynamics is governed by the Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\label{eq: model} \begin{aligned} H= H_d+H_leads+H_tun = \ce_d\sum_\sigma d_{\sigma}^\dag d_{\sigma}+\sum_{p\sigma\alpha} \ce_{p\alpha} c_{p\sigma\alpha}^\dag c_{p\sigma\alpha} +\sum_{p\sigma\alpha}(V_{p\alpha} d_{\sigma}^\dag c_{p\sigma\alpha}+\rm{H.c.}). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Here, the $d_{\sigma}$ operators represent the modes of spin $\sigma$ with onsite energy $\ce_d$ at the impurity site.}
elements.formula-2411-002: \begin{equation}\label{eq: model}  H= H_d+H_leads+H_tun = \ce_d\sum_\sigma d_{\sigma}^\dag d_{\sigma}+\sum_{p\sigma\alpha} \ce_{p\alpha} c_{p\sigma\alpha}^\dag c_{p\sigma\alpha} +\sum_{p\sigma\alpha}(V_{p\alpha} d_{\sigma}^\dag c_{p\sigma\alpha}+\rm{H.c.}). \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-040: In the spinful fermionic bath, the $c_{p\sigma\alpha}$ operators correspond to the eigenmodes of channel $\alpha$, which have single-particle energies $\ce_{p\alpha =\ce_p-\mu_\alpha$ with momentum $p$ and chemical potential $\mu_\alpha$.}
elements.sent-2411-041: Fermions at the impurity site can tunnel into the bath via the hopping term $H_\rm{tun}$ with channel-dependent amplitudes $V_{p \alpha}$.
elements.sent-2411-042: The dissipative part of the dynamics in Eq.~\eqref{eq: master equation is described by the jump operator $L= d_{\downarrow d_{\uparrow}$, which represents two-body loss on the impurity site.}}
elements.sent-2411-043: It is worth noting that our model is related to the well-known Anderson impurity model (AIM) described by the Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\label{eq: AIM} H_\rm{AIM}=\ce_d\sum_\sigma d_{\sigma}^\dag d_{\sigma} + \frac{U_d}{2} d^\dag_\uparrow d_\uparrow d^\dag_\downarrow d_\downarrow + H_leads+H_tun,\end{equation} where the impurity instead has onsite Coulomb repulsion with interaction strength $U_d$.
elements.formula-2411-003: \begin{equation}\label{eq: AIM} H_\rm{AIM}=\ce_d\sum_\sigma d_{\sigma}^\dag d_{\sigma} + \frac{U_d}{2} d^\dag_\uparrow d_\uparrow d^\dag_\downarrow d_\downarrow + H_leads+H_tun,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-044: In the AIM, a strong onsite repulsion generates correlations by imposing a constraint of no double occupancy on the impurity site.
elements.sent-2411-045: In our model \eqref{eq: master equation , we achieve the same constraint by introducing a dissipation that actively removes double occupancies.}
elements.sent-2411-046: However, we stress that in our model there are no interactions; all the correlations are introduced by two-body losses.
elements.sent-2411-047: In this section, we introduce and motivate the SGS variational ansatz in the context of closed systems.
elements.sent-2411-048: We then extend this method to open-system impurity models by using the quantum trajectory approach.
elements.sent-2411-049: The main achievement of our work is the combination of SGS with quantum trajectory, whose analytical details are presented in Sec.
elements.sent-2411-050: We begin by introducing the SGS ansatz for closed systems.
elements.sent-2411-051: Considering the AIM with Hamiltonian \eqref{eq: AIM , the hybridization between the impurity and the bath leads to charge fluctuations at the impurity site.}
elements.sent-2411-052: A general wave function can be expressed as a superposition of four states that feature different configurations in the impurity subspace, namely \begin{equation}\label{eq: a general state} \ket{\Psi}=\sum_{s=0, \uparrow, \downarrow, 2} \alpha_s \ket{s}\ket{\psi_s}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-053: Here, $\ket{s}$ denotes the impurity states $\ket{0}$ (vacuum state), $\ket{\uparrow}$, $\ket{\downarrow}$, or $\ket{2}$ (double occupation), and $\ket{\psi_s}$ represents the corresponding many-body state of the bath.
elements.sent-2411-054: The coefficients $\alpha_s$ capture the entanglement between the impurity and the bath.
elements.sent-2411-055: To gain intuition about the structure of the bath states $\ket{\psi_s}$, we first consider the case when the tunneling $H_\rm{tun}$ is turned off and the bath is in its ground state, the Fermi sea $\ket{\rm{FS }$.}
elements.sent-2411-056: When the tunneling is turned on, the bath becomes connected to the impurity.
elements.sent-2411-057: As $H_\rm{tun}$ transfers electrons into and out of the bath, it generates both charge fluctuations and particle-hole excitations in the bath.
elements.sent-2411-058: This idea is the basis of the Gunnarson-Sch\"onhammer ansatz, which was successfully employed to predict both the ground-state properties and the spectral function of the AIM \cite{Gunnarsson1983 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: A simple way to further improve the ansatz is to assume Gaussian states for the baths within different impurity subsectors, namely an ansatz using a superposition of four Gaussian states: \begin{equation}\label{eq: SGS ansatz} \ket{\Psi_{\rm{SGS}}}=\sum_{s=0, \uparrow, \downarrow, 2} \alpha_s\ket{s}\ket{\rm{GS}_s},\end{equation} which increases the number of variational parameters while remaining numerically efficiently.
elements.sent-2411-060: Here, the Gaussian state $\ket{\rm{GS_s }$ corresponds to a single Slater determinant and can capture the particle-hole excitations non-perturbatively, which will be precisely defined in Sec.~\ref{sec: SGS techniques .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: To describe closed-system dynamics, we consider $\alpha$ and $\ket{\rm{GS _s}$ to be time-dependent.}
elements.sent-2411-062: The technical details of implementing the real-time variational SGS ansatz are also discussed in Sec.~\ref{sec: SGS techniques .}
elements.sent-2411-063: It should be noted that Bravyi and Gosset \cite{BravyiGosset_complexity have shown that the ground state of an impurity Hamiltonian, up to a small fixed error, can be approximated as a superposition of a few Gaussian states, with the number of states scaling polynomially with the size of the bath.}
elements.sent-2411-064: The SGS ansatz \eqref{eq: SGS ansatz used in our work can be considered as a ``minimal version" of the most general SGS and thus highly efficient.}
elements.sent-2411-065: The variational SGS has been implemented in closed systems and has been shown to capture both ground-state \cite{boutin2021quantum and dynamical properties \cite{nondissipativeSGS .}}
elements.sent-2411-066: The SGS ansatz works well with pure states, both in and out of equilibrium.
elements.sent-2411-067: For our dissipative problem---and more generally---we aim to further extend it to open-system impurity problems.
elements.sent-2411-068: The simplest way is to employ the quantum trajectory approach, which allows approximating a mixed state with an ensemble of pure states.
elements.sent-2411-069: This enables the direct use Eq.~\eqref{eq: SGS ansatz .}

elements.subsection-2411-002: Quantum trajectory approach\label{sec: quantum trajectory approach}
elements.sent-2411-071: In this subsection, to make our work self-contained, we briefly revisit the quantum trajectory approach, previously detailed in the literature \cite{Dalibard1992,Molmer1993,Dum1992,Dum1992_2,carmichael1993open .}
elements.sent-2411-072: Considering the master equation \eqref{eq: master equation , we rewrite it as \begin{equation}\label{eq: general master equation} \dt \rho = - i (H_\rm{NH}\,\rho - \rho\,H_\rm{NH}^\dag) + \gamma L^\dag \rho L,\end{equation} with a non-Hermitian Hamiltonian \begin{equation}\label{eq: non-Hermitian Ham} H_\rm{NH} = H-i\frac{\gamma}{2}L^\dag L.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-006: \begin{equation}\label{eq: general master equation} \dt \rho = - i (H_\rm{NH}\,\rho - \rho\,H_\rm{NH}^\dag) + \gamma L^\dag \rho L,\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-073: It has been proven that Eq.~\eqref{eq: general master equation can be reproduced by the following unraveling procedure.}
elements.sent-2411-074: For simplicity, we assume that the initial density operator $\rho(t=0)=\ketbra{\psi_0}$ is a pure state, with $\ket{\psi_0}$ denoting the normalized initial wave function.
elements.sent-2411-075: Then, one forms an ensemble of $N_\rm{Traj}$ quantum trajectories $\ket{\psi_i(t)}$ by evolving $\ket{\psi_0}$ with $H_\rm{NH}$, and interspersing the evolution with discontinuous quantum jumps $\ket{\psi_i(t^+) =L\ket{\psi_i(t^-) /\norm{L\ket{\psi_i(t^-) }$ according to certain probabilistic rules.}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: The average state of the ensemble thus constructed, \begin{equation}\label{eq: density operator with QT} \rho(t)=\frac{1}{N_\rm{Traj}}\sum_{i=1}^{N_\rm{Traj}} \frac{\ketbra{\psi_i(t)}}{\braket{\psi_i(t)}{\psi_i(t)}},\end{equation} converges to the solution of Eq.~\eqref{eq: general master equation as $N_\rm{Traj \rightarrow \infty$.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: In a concrete simulation with a finite $N_\rm{Traj}$, Eq.~\eqref{eq: density operator with QT provides an approximation to the desired $\rho(t)$.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Following Ref.~\cite{DaleyQuantumTrajectories , we outline the main procedures on how to obtain $\ket{\psi_i(t)}$: (a) Draw a uniformly distributed random number $r$ from $[0,1]$.}
elements.sent-2411-079: The expectation value of an observable $\hat{O}$ is obtained by taking the average over the contributions from all trajectories, namely \begin{equation}O(t)\equiv\rm{Tr}(\hat{O}\rho(t))=\frac{1}{N_\rm{Traj}}\sum_{i=1}^{N_\rm{Traj}} O_i(t),\end{equation} with $O_i(t) \equiv \bra{\psi_i(t)}\hat{O}\ket{\psi_i(t) /\braket{\psi_i(t) {\psi_i(t)}$ being the expectation value for a single trajectory $i$.}}
elements.sent-2411-080: For large but finite $N_\rm{Traj}$, the statistical error of $O(t)$ \begin{equation}\sigma_O=\frac{\Delta O}{\sqrt{N_\rm{Traj}}}\end{equation} asymptotically approaches zero, as $\sigma_O\sim N_\rm{Traj ^{-1/2}$.}
elements.sent-2411-081: Here, $\Delta O=\sum_i(O_i-O)^2/N_\rm{Traj}$ is the standard deviation, which generally converges as $N_\rm{Traj}\rightarrow\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-082: The quantum trajectory approach has the advantage of evolving pure states of dimension $N_\mathcal{H}$ instead of the density operator of dimension $N_\mathcal{H ^2$, where the Hilbert space dimension $N_\mathcal{H}$ generally increases exponentially with the size of the system.}
elements.sent-2411-083: Therefore, it is very efficient for solving open-system quantum problems.
elements.sent-2411-084: The trade-off is the introduction of statistical errors resulting from the (classical) fluctuations in the sampling of a finite number of trajectories.
elements.sent-2411-085: Nevertheless, there is no sign problem in this approach (each trajectory carries equal and positive weight), so the errors can be well controlled by increasing the number of trajectories.

elements.subsection-2411-005: Spin relaxation: crossover from the mean-field regime to the Kondo regime
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{Figures/Relaxation_1.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.8\linewidth]{Figures/Relaxation_2.pdf} \caption{ Spin relaxation. (a): the decay rate $\Gamma^S_\rm{Decay}$ (extracted from dynamics in the inset) versus dissipation rate $\gamma$. In the large $\gamma$ regime, the relaxation rate $\Gamma^S_\rm{Decay}$ converges, displaying a plateau as depicted by the dashed curve. This plateau indicates the Kondo regime with two-body losses. In the small $\gamma$ regime, the SGS results closely align with the mean-field results (dotted line). The inset shows the real time dynamics of the impurity magnetization $\langle \sigma_{d}^z\rangle=\langle n_{d\uparrow}\rangle - \langle n_{d\downarrow}\rangle$, which exhibits decay behavior $\langle \sigma_{d}^z\rangle\sim e^{-\Gamma^S_\rm{Decay}t}$ at large times. The system size for the SGS simulations is set to $\ell=60$, with $\ce_d=-0.5J$. (b): spin decay rate versus $\ce_d$. The collapse of different curves shows the universal behavior (at large dissipation) $\Gamma^S_\rm{Decay}\sim \exp (-\eta \pi|\ce_d|/\Gamma)$ with a coefficient $\eta\sim0.61$, while the RG prediction at $\gamma=\infty$ is $\eta_\rm{RG}=1$ \cite{Coleman}. The discrepancy results from the renormalization of the effective spin interaction between the impurity site fermion and itinerant fermions by higher-order processes in $\Gamma/\ce_d$. } \label{fig:spin-relaxation}
elements.sent-2411-163: We now focus on the first Kondo signature: impurity’s spin relaxation slows down with increasing $\gamma$ \cite{short_paper ; in the Kondo regime, the lifetime of the impurity's magnetization is $1/T_K$, where $T_K$ is the Kondo temperature.}
elements.sent-2411-164: To capture spin relaxation with the SGS approach, we consider a larger system ($\ell=60$) with an onsite potential $\ce_d<0$.
elements.sent-2411-165: The chemical potentials are fixed to $\mu_L=\mu_R=0$, so the system is around half filling and only the symmetric modes need to be considered.
elements.sent-2411-166: To simulate the dynamics, we first turn off both dissipation and tunneling $H_\rm{tun}$, i.e., set $\gamma=V=0$.
elements.sent-2411-167: The system is prepared as a disentangled static state $\ket{\uparrow}\ket{\rm{FS }$, combining a spin-up impurity and the Fermi sea of the bath.}
elements.sent-2411-168: Then we suddenly turn on both $\gamma$ and $V$, and the magnetization of the impurity, denoted by $\langle \sigma^z_d\rangle = \langle n_{d\uparrow}\rangle - \langle n_{d\downarrow}\rangle$, starts relaxing toward the equilibrium value of zero.
elements.sent-2411-169: In the inset panel of Fig.~\ref{fig:spin-relaxation (a), we show the evolution of the logarithm of $\langle \sigma^z_d\rangle$ with different dissipation rates $\gamma$.}
elements.sent-2411-170: The dynamics exhibits two stages: the first stage is a universal decrease with the total tunneling rate $\Gamma\equiv\Gamma(\omega=0)=4V^2/J$, which is mainly due to the single-particle tunneling of charge to the bath.
elements.sent-2411-171: The second stage for $t>4 J^{-1}$ is a linear curve, i.e., an exponential decay of $\langle \sigma^z_d\rangle$, with a rate dependent on $\gamma$, which is fitted by using $\ln \langle \sigma^z_d\rangle\sim -\Gamma^S_\rm{Decay t + \mathcal{C}$ with the fitting parameter $\Gamma^S_\rm{Decay}$ representing the $\gamma$-dependent relaxation rate.}
elements.sent-2411-172: We emphasize here that the evolution with $\gamma=\infty$ is unitary due to the quantum Zeno effect, hence without quantum trajectories, and it is equivalent to that of the AIM with $U_d=\infty$, in which the late-time spin relaxation is governed by the Kondo temperature $T_K\sim \sqrt{|\ce_d| \Gamma}\exp (- \pi|\ce_d|/\Gamma)$ \cite{Coleman .}

elements.sent-2411-174: Moreover, in the high dissipation regime ($\gamma>10^2J$), $\Gamma^S_\rm{Decay}$ slowly converges to a small but finite value, which we will demonstrate to be the Kondo temperature.
elements.sent-2411-175: Therefore, the convergence of $\Gamma^S_\rm{Decay}$ is a clear signature of the Kondo regime.
elements.sent-2411-176: In the small dissipation regime ($\gamma \lesssim 2J$), dynamics can be treated in a mean-field (MF) approximation (cf.

elements.sent-2411-179: We now demonstrate that the value of $\Gamma^S_\rm{Decay}$ as $\gamma\rightarrow\infty$ in Fig.~\ref{fig:spin-relaxation (a) is proportional to the Kondo temperature.}

elements.sent-2411-181: This collapse shows a universal behavior of the spin relaxation rate (or the Kondo temperature) $\Gamma^S_\rm{Decay}\sim \exp (-\eta \pi|\ce_d|/\Gamma)$ with a fitting coefficient $\eta\sim0.61$.

elements.sent-2411-318: In this work, we introduced a non-perturbative framework that combines the SGS variational ansatz with the quantum trajectory approach, offering an efficient method for simulating dissipative impurity systems.
elements.sent-2411-319: We applied this method to a system where a spinful impurity, subject to two-body losses, interacts with a bath of noninteracting fermions.
elements.sent-2411-320: Through this approach, we demonstrated that strong localized two-body losses can induce the Kondo effect, consistent with findings in related research \cite{short_paper .}
elements.sent-2411-321: The key signatures of Kondo physics, such as the slowdown of spin relaxation and the enhancement of charge conductance, were effectively captured using the SGS framework.
elements.sent-2411-322: This method allowed us to thoroughly examine the full crossover from the weakly correlated mean-field regime at small dissipation to the strongly correlated Kondo regime at large dissipation, thereby providing a comprehensive understanding of the system’s behavior across different dissipation regimes.
elements.sent-2411-323: Notably, in the mean-field regime, we revealed the emergence of exotic ``reverse conductance" at zero potential bias, which arises from intermediate dissipation and the constraints of a finite reservoir bandwidth.
elements.sent-2411-324: Our framework can be extended to more complex scenarios, including systems with multiple dissipative impurity sites or involving a superconducting or Mott-like fermionic baths.
elements.sent-2411-325: Our exploration of dissipative Kondo models suggests new directions for utilizing localized dissipation to potentially realize large-scale, tunable spin-1/2 and higher-spin Kondo systems in cold-atom setups.
elements.sent-2411-326: These systems could serve as a basis for investigating strongly correlated phenomena within the broader Kondo problem family, such as quantum criticality \cite{Jones1988,Affleck1992,Affleck1993,bayat2014,keller2015,iftikhar2015,iftikhar2018,lorenzo2017quantum,pouse2023 , fractionalization \cite{Emery1992,Gan1995,Sela2011,Mitchell2012,Mitchell2012_2,Landau2018,Karki2023 , and multistage Kondo effects \cite{Hofstetter2001,Pustilnik2001,Wiel2002,Granger2005,Karki2018,guo2021 .}}}
